reason: CISC = Complex Instruction Set Computer (standard acronym).
NAND is functionally complete (universal gate); AND and OR alone are not.
Multiplexer: 2^n data inputs selected onto one output; demux/decoder have one input and many outputs.
Address seen by the memory unit / main memory is the physical (real) address; logical is CPU-generated.
A flip-flop is the basic 1-bit storage element; a register stores multiple bits.
DBMS = Database Management System.
A DBMS is software that stores, modifies and retrieves data; it is not a language or a set of queries.
Hierarchical, network and distributed are recognized database types; 'decentralized' is not a standard DB classification.
Data, data languages (DDL/DML) and the data manager are all DBMS components.
SELECT/INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE on tuples is DML; DDL defines schema, DCL grants, TCL commits.

Real fault: ID is in SELECT but neither grouped nor aggregated. Option texts are garbled ('Dept_id' appears nowhere in the query); A is the only option gesturing at the select/group-by mismatch, so it is the intended key, but the option wording is defective and should be rewritten.
DML = Data Manipulation Language.

Defective as written: minimum redundancy, high security and ACID support are ALL genuine DBMS features, so three options are correct. Stem was almost certainly 'Which is NOT a feature of DBMS?' (answer C, single-user access only). Needs stem repair.
Databases provide a user/query interface; no-backup and lack of authentication are anti-features, and multi-location storage is specific to distributed DBs, not a general database feature.
Relational model organizes data as a collection of tables (relations).
Option D omits the FROM clause, so it is syntactically invalid.
FAT = File Allocation Table.
Loadable kernel modules can be inserted/removed at runtime, so B is the false statement; A, C, D are true of kernels.
OS X's XNU combines Mach microkernel with BSD monolithic components = hybrid kernel.
A process owns its address space/globals, its open-file table, and its pending signals/alarms and handlers (these are per-process, not per-thread).
